---
title: "Choosing smoothing parameters: the Fellner–Schall method"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
Several rungs of [the ladder](index.qmd) add a **penalty** to the weighted
least-squares solve from the [GLM chapter](glm.qmd): a smooth's wiggliness
penalty (GAM), a random effect's variance (LME), a kernel prior (HSGP). Each
penalty carries a strength $\lambda$ that must be *chosen*, not guessed.
**Fellner–Schall** is the cheap update that chooses it:

$$
\lambda_k \;\leftarrow\; \lambda_k \,
\frac{\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-} S_k) - \operatorname{tr}(V S_k)}
     {\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / \phi}.
$$

It is **multiplicative** (so $\lambda$ stays positive), it **increases the
restricted marginal likelihood every step**, and — because a roughness penalty
*is* a random-effect variance (the [equivalence](#equivalence) shown below) —
the *same* update selects smoothing parameters and estimates variance
components. That reuse is why it recurs across the suite. The single new idea
this page adds: the update above is the **REML score equation for $\lambda$,
rearranged into a fixed point**. Code:
[`nitrix/stats/gam.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gam.py).
:::

This is a **shared-machinery** chapter, not a model of its own: Fellner–Schall is
one algorithm the suite reuses. It sits directly on top of the [GLM](glm.qmd)
solve — add a penalty to those weighted normal equations and you immediately need
a principled way to set the penalty's strength; this is that way. We introduce it
in its most natural home, the smoothing penalty of a generalised additive model,
and the mixed-model and Gaussian-process rungs reuse it unchanged.

## The problem every smooth shares {#fellner-schall}

Fit, per element (voxel, vertex, fixel), a generalised additive model

$$
g\!\left(\mathbb{E}[y]\right) \;=\;
X\beta \;+\; \sum_k f_k(x_k),
\qquad f_k(x_k) = B_k(x_k)\,\gamma_k,
$$ {#eq-gam}

where each smooth $f_k$ is a spline expanded in a basis $B_k$ with coefficients
$\gamma_k$ (see `nitrix.stats.basis`). Left unconstrained, a flexible basis
overfits. So each smooth carries a **roughness penalty**

$$
\operatorname{pen}_k(\gamma_k) = \lambda_k\, \gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k,
$$ {#eq-penalty}

with a fixed penalty matrix $S_k$ (a differencing or curvature operator) and a
**smoothing parameter** $\lambda_k \ge 0$ that we must *choose*. Small
$\lambda_k$ → wiggly; large $\lambda_k$ → stiff. Everything below is about
choosing $\lambda_k$ well, cheaply, for millions of elements at once.

Given the $\lambda_k$, fitting the coefficients is the easy part: penalised
iteratively-reweighted least squares (a weighted normal-equations solve with the
block penalty $S_\lambda = \sum_k \lambda_k S_k$ added). In `nitrix` this inner
loop is cuSOLVER-free (`linalg._smalllinalg.small_inv_logdet`) so it runs on
accelerators where the vendor solver is unavailable — and, as the
[solvers chapter](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky) shows, the *same* Cholesky factor
that solves the system hands us the log-determinant and traces the outer loop
needs for free. The interesting question is the **outer loop**.

::: {.callout-note title="A concrete question to keep in mind"}
You have cortical-thickness maps for $N=120$ subjects and want, *per vertex*, the
nonlinear trajectory of thickness against age — a smooth $f(\text{age})$. Too
small a $\lambda$ and the curve chases noise (spurious "atrophy then regrowth");
too large and it flattens to a line, hiding a real adolescent peak. You cannot
eyeball $\lambda$ for 300{,}000 vertices. You need an estimator that reads the
right stiffness *off the data at each vertex*, cheaply enough to run everywhere at
once. That estimator is what this page derives.
:::

## Why penalties are variances {#equivalence}

Here is the observation the whole suite is built on, and the reason one update
serves so many models.

::: {#thm-equivalence}
## Penalty–variance-component equivalence
Fitting the smooth @eq-gam with the quadratic penalty @eq-penalty by penalised
least squares is identical to computing the posterior mean of a Gaussian
linear mixed model in which the coefficients carry the (possibly improper)
Gaussian prior
$\gamma_k \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \sigma_{b,k}^2 S_k^{-}\right)$, with the
correspondence
$$
\lambda_k \;=\; \frac{\phi}{\sigma_{b,k}^2},
$$ {#eq-lambda-ratio}
where $\phi$ is the model dispersion and $\sigma_{b,k}^2$ the random-effect
variance.
:::

::: {.proof}
The penalised least-squares objective
$\lVert y - B\gamma\rVert^2/\phi + \sum_k \lambda_k \gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k$
is, term for term, $-2\log$ of the Gaussian posterior
$p(\gamma \mid y) \propto \exp\!\big(-\lVert y-B\gamma\rVert^2/2\phi\big)\,
\prod_k \exp\!\big(-\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / 2\sigma_{b,k}^2\big)$
once we set $\lambda_k = \phi/\sigma_{b,k}^2$. The maximiser of a Gaussian
log-posterior is its mean, so the penalised fit and the mixed-model posterior
mean coincide. $\square$
:::

@thm-equivalence is the hinge. "Choose the smoothing parameter $\lambda_k$" and
"estimate the random-effect variance $\sigma_{b,k}^2$" are the *same task* read
in two dialects, formalised the same way in the
[foundations chapter](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence). Choose your estimator for
one and you have an estimator for the other.

Which estimator? We could maximise the *ordinary* likelihood over
$(\beta,\theta)$ jointly, but that is known to underestimate variances: it spends
degrees of freedom fitting $\beta$ and then judges the residual scatter as if
$\beta$ had been free. The fix — explained in full in the
[LME chapter](lme.qmd#sec-reml) — is **restricted** maximum likelihood (REML):
integrate $\beta$ out of the likelihood first, then maximise what remains over the
variances $\theta$. REML is the estimator `nitrix` uses, and the update that
climbs the REML surface in $\lambda$ is Fellner–Schall.

## Deriving the Fellner–Schall update {#the-update}

[]{#sec-fs-update}

This is the heart of the page. We will *build* the update from the REML score
rather than quote it, so that by the end you could write it down yourself.

### What we are differentiating

Write the marginal (observation) covariance of one element's response as
$V_y = ZGZ^{\top} + R$, with $R = \phi I$ the residual covariance and
$G = \sigma_b^2 S^{-}$ the prior covariance of the smooth coefficients
(@thm-equivalence). The REML objective — the log restricted likelihood, with
$\beta$ profiled out — is, up to constants,

$$
2\,\ell_R(\theta)
= -\log\lvert V_y\rvert
  -\log\bigl\lvert X^{\top} V_y^{-1} X\bigr\rvert
  - r^{\top} V_y^{-1} r,
\qquad r = y - X\hat\beta .
$$ {#eq-reml-obj}

We want $\partial \ell_R / \partial \sigma_b^2 = 0$. Differentiating a
log-determinant and an inverse are the two identities every variance-component
derivation leans on:
$\partial \log\lvert V_y\rvert = \operatorname{tr}(V_y^{-1}\,\partial V_y)$ and
$\partial V_y^{-1} = -V_y^{-1}(\partial V_y)V_y^{-1}$. Since
$\partial V_y / \partial \sigma_b^2 = ZSZ^{\top}\!$ ... but here is the move that
makes everything cheap: rather than work in the $N\times N$ observation space, we
push the whole thing into the small $p\times p$ **coefficient** space, where it
becomes a statement about the penalty matrix $S$ directly.

### From the score to a fixed point

Let us carry that move through for a single smoothing parameter, working
directly in $\lambda_k$. By @thm-equivalence the penalty contributes
$\lambda_k S_k$ to the penalised Hessian $A = X^{\top}WX + S_\lambda$ whose
inverse is the coefficient covariance $V$. Two facts let us write the restricted
log-likelihood's $\lambda_k$-derivative without ever touching the $N\times N$
space. First, the term that *rewards* flexibility is the prior normaliser
$-\tfrac12\log\lvert S_\lambda\rvert_{+}$ (the penalty's own log-determinant,
pseudo-determinant where $S_\lambda$ is rank-deficient); its derivative is
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-} S_k)$ — the *rank-like* term, "how much
penalty is on the table." Second, the term that *pays* for flexibility is the
Hessian normaliser $-\tfrac12\log\lvert A\rvert$ plus the realised penalty
$-\tfrac{\lambda_k}{2}\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k/\phi$; differentiating the
log-determinant via $\partial\log\lvert A\rvert = \operatorname{tr}(A^{-1}\partial A)
= \operatorname{tr}(V S_k)$ gives the cost as
$\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}(V S_k) + \tfrac12\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k/\phi$.
(The $\beta$-profiling term in @eq-reml-obj contributes the same trace structure
and is folded into $\operatorname{tr}(V S_k)$ once $V$ is the *penalised* Hessian
inverse — this is the bookkeeping that the coefficient-space move tidies away.)

Collecting these, the restricted score in $\lambda_k$ is

$$
\frac{\partial \ell_R}{\partial \lambda_k}
= \tfrac12\Bigl[\operatorname{tr}\!\left(S_\lambda^{-} S_k\right)
  - \operatorname{tr}\!\left(V S_k\right)
  - \lambda_k\,\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / \phi\Bigr]
  \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_k},
$$ {#eq-fs-score}

The bracket vanishes at the optimum, so the stationarity condition is

$$
\operatorname{tr}\!\left(S_\lambda^{-} S_k\right) - \operatorname{tr}\!\left(V S_k\right)
= \lambda_k\,\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / \phi.
$$ {#eq-fs-stationary}

Read the right-hand side carefully: the unknown $\lambda_k$ appears *both*
explicitly as the leading factor *and* implicitly inside every trace and inside
$\gamma_k$ (they were all computed at the current $\lambda_k$). We cannot solve
that in closed form. What we *can* do is freeze the implicit dependence — hold
the traces and $\gamma_k$ at their current values — and read @eq-fs-stationary
as an equation for one fresh $\lambda_k$. The Fellner–Schall choice is the
*multiplicative* freezing: keep the explicit $\lambda_k$ on the right as the
*old* iterate and let the left-hand ratio rescale it,

$$
\lambda_k^{\text{new}}
= \lambda_k \cdot
\frac{\operatorname{tr}\!\left(S_\lambda^{-} S_k\right) - \operatorname{tr}\!\left(V S_k\right)}
     {\lambda_k\,\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / \phi}
= \lambda_k \cdot
\frac{\operatorname{tr}\!\left(S_\lambda^{-} S_k\right) - \operatorname{tr}\!\left(V S_k\right)}
     {\gamma_k^{\top}\!\left(\lambda_k S_k\right)\gamma_k / \phi}.
$$ {#eq-fs-rearrange}

The denominator's $\lambda_k$ cancels the leading one, leaving the canonical form
$\lambda_k^{\text{new}} = \lambda_k\,[\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-}S_k) -
\operatorname{tr}(VS_k)]/(\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k/\phi)$ used in the code.
Why *this* freezing rather than the direct solve
$\lambda_k = [\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-}S_k)-\operatorname{tr}(VS_k)]
/(\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k/\phi)$? Because the multiplicative form rescales a
*positive* old iterate by a *positive* ratio (the numerator is the effective
degrees of freedom, the denominator a sum of squares), so it can never step to a
negative variance — the property we lean on below. At a fixed point the traces
and $\gamma_k$ stop moving, the rescaling factor is exactly $1$, and
@eq-fs-rearrange reproduces $\partial\ell_R/\partial\lambda_k = 0$. The next
subsection re-reads this same ratio as a contest between two intuitive
quantities.

### The two competing forces

The algebra of @eq-fs-rearrange is exact, but the *reason* it works is easier to
hold as a picture. The score @eq-fs-score is a balance between two scalar
quantities; once you see what each one measures, the update is self-evidently the
right rescaling. Stated as an idea first:

- **What the penalty bought you.** A finite $\lambda$ does not freeze the smooth
  rigidly; it lets the data pull some wiggliness back. The amount it pulls back is
  exactly the *effective degrees of freedom* the smooth spends — and that number,
  it turns out, is a trace: how much of the penalty's available "rigidity"
  ($\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-} S_k)$, what you *could* constrain) is left
  unspent after the posterior covariance $V$ absorbs some of it
  ($\operatorname{tr}(V S_k)$, what the data *did* loosen). The difference,
  $\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-} S_k) - \operatorname{tr}(V S_k)$, is a
  degrees-of-freedom term: *how much smoothing the data actually used*.

- **The roughness you actually have.** Given the fitted coefficients $\gamma_k$,
  the realised roughness of the smooth is the quadratic form
  $\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k$ — a single number measuring how wiggly the chosen
  curve really is, scaled by the dispersion $\phi$.

Setting the REML derivative to zero (@eq-fs-score) says these two must balance.
Solving the linear-in-$\lambda_k$ part for the *new* $\lambda_k$, exactly as in
@eq-fs-rearrange, gives the Fellner–Schall update in its canonical form

$$
\lambda_k \;\leftarrow\; \lambda_k \,
\frac{\operatorname{tr}\!\left(S_\lambda^{-} S_k\right) - \operatorname{tr}\!\left(V S_k\right)}
     {\gamma_k^{\top} S_k \gamma_k / \phi},
\qquad
V = \left(X^{\top} W X + S_\lambda\right)^{-1},
$$ {#eq-fellner-schall}

where $V$ is the Bayesian coefficient covariance and $W$ the IRLS weights from the
[GLM working model](glm.qmd#sec-working). This is the Fellner–Schall update. Read
it as: *next stiffness = current stiffness × (smoothing the data used) / (roughness
the data has)*. If the smooth used more degrees of freedom than its current
roughness justifies, the ratio exceeds one and $\lambda_k$ grows (stiffer); if it
used fewer, $\lambda_k$ shrinks (more flexible). The fixed point is reached when
the wiggliness the model *spent* equals the wiggliness it *has* — which is exactly
the REML score equation $\partial \ell_R/\partial\sigma_{b,k}^2 = 0$ in disguise,
multiplied through by $\lambda_k$ so that it reads as a rescaling.

### Why it has these three properties

Three things make @eq-fellner-schall the right tool, not just *a* tool:

1. **Multiplicative ⇒ positivity-preserving.** $\lambda_k$ is *rescaled*, never
   *shifted*. A Newton or gradient step on $\sigma_b^2$ can overshoot past zero
   into a meaningless negative variance, forcing you to clip or project. Here a
   positive $\lambda_k$ times a positive ratio is positive — automatically, with
   no boundary logic in the hot path. (The LME chapter's
   [Newton solver](lme.qmd#sec-reml) instead optimises in $\log\sigma^2$ for the
   same reason; Fellner–Schall gets positivity for free in the *natural*
   parameter.)
2. **Ascent.** Each step increases the (Laplace) restricted marginal likelihood
   under mild conditions, so it is a *monotone* fixed-point iteration — it cannot
   thrash. A fixed iteration count therefore converges reliably, and a fixed count
   is exactly what a `vmap`-clean, JIT-compiled per-element fit needs (`nitrix`
   uses `n_outer = 20` outer steps by default in `gam_fit`, inside a `lax.scan`).
   Convergence is *honest, not magic*: it is geometric near the optimum and can be
   slow when a smooth is nearly linear (the data want $\lambda \to \infty$), which
   is why `gam_fit` clamps $\lambda$ to `[lam_floor, lam_ceil]` = $[10^{-6},
   10^{8}]$ rather than chasing a boundary forever.
3. **Cheap — only traces of things you already factorised.** Every quantity in
   @eq-fellner-schall is read off the *same* penalised Hessian
   $X^{\top}WX + S_\lambda$ the inner solve already inverted. No new system is
   formed. The denominator is a quadratic form in the coefficients you just
   computed; the numerator is two traces. The
   [Cholesky machinery](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky) that produced $V$ also produces
   the log-determinant, so the outer step is essentially free relative to the
   inner solve.

### The disjoint-block shortcut

The numerator hides a generalised inverse $S_\lambda^{-}$ of the *summed* penalty
$S_\lambda = \sum_j \lambda_j S_j$ — an object we would rather never form. For GAM
smooths it collapses, because each smooth occupies its own coefficient block.

::: {#lem-disjoint}
## Disjoint-block trace
If the smooths occupy disjoint coefficient blocks then
$\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-} S_k) = \operatorname{rank}(S_k)/\lambda_k$,
so no generalised inverse of $S_\lambda = \sum_j \lambda_j S_j$ is ever formed.
:::

::: {.proof}
On disjoint blocks $S_\lambda$ is block-diagonal, so its pseudo-inverse is the
block-diagonal of the per-block pseudo-inverses; the cross term $S_\lambda^{-}S_k$
is supported only on block $k$, where $S_\lambda = \lambda_k S_k$ and thus
$S_\lambda^{-}S_k = \lambda_k^{-1} S_k^{-}S_k$, the (scaled) projector onto the
range of $S_k$. Its trace is $\operatorname{rank}(S_k)/\lambda_k$. $\square$
:::

In `nitrix` this trace is computed by `_trace_slinv_sk` in `gam.py`, which is
slightly more general than the lemma: it works from the *block eigenvalues*
`pen_eig` of each penalty in the joint eigenbasis,
$\operatorname{tr}(S_\lambda^{-}S_k)
 = \sum_i [\mu_i > 0]\, e_{k,i}/\mu_i$, where $\mu_i = (\lambda\!\cdot\!\texttt{pen\_eig})_i$.
For a lone disjoint penalty this is *exactly* $\operatorname{rank}(S_k)/\lambda_k$
(the lemma); for **overlapping** tensor-product penalties — a tensor smooth
carries one $\lambda$ per margin, sharing coefficients — it is the correct general
trace with still no pseudo-inverse materialised. That collapse is the line between
"elegant on paper" and "runs per-voxel on a GPU."

Putting the two loops together — this maps one-to-one onto `_gam_fit_one` in
`gam.py`:

::: {.algorithm}
**Algorithm 1 — Generalised Fellner–Schall smoothing-parameter selection**

```
input:  data y, design X, smooth bases {B_k}, penalties {S_k}, block eigs {e_k}
init:   λ_k ← 1                                  for all k
repeat (n_outer = 20, a fixed lax.scan):
    # inner: penalised IRLS at the current λ  (cuSOLVER-free WLS solve)
    S_λ ← Σ_k λ_k S_k
    (β, γ), V, XᵀWX ← penalised_irls(y, X, {B_k}, S_λ)   # n_inner = 10
    φ              ← Gaussian: rss / (N − tr(V XᵀWX));  else fixed
    # outer: one Fellner–Schall step per smooth
    for each smooth k:
        num   ← λ_k · tr(S_λ⁻ S_k) − λ_k · tr(V S_k)     # via block eigenvalues
        den   ← (γ_kᵀ S_k γ_k) / φ
        λ_k   ← clip(num / den, lam_floor, lam_ceil)
return β, γ, λ, V, effective degrees of freedom (edf_k = block-trace of V XᵀWX)
```
:::

The Bayesian covariance $V$ that falls out later yields per-smooth **effective
degrees of freedom** $\operatorname{edf}_k$ and the confidence bands and
approximate $F$/$\chi^2$ tests reported in `GAMResult`. For the Gaussian identity
link `nitrix` adds two fast paths that change *nothing* about the update: the
per-voxel cross-product path (`_gam_fit_one_gaussian_xprod`), where the whole
outer loop runs in $p$-space with no $N$-vector in the loop, and the pooled
`lambda_mode='shared'` path (`_gam_fit_shared_gaussian`), where one $\lambda$ is
selected across all elements from $(p\times p)$ sufficient statistics in
$O(\texttt{n\_outer}\,p^3)$ — independent of the number of voxels.

## The REML / EM connection — one loop for the whole GAMM {#sec-reml-em}

It is worth stating plainly *why* this one update does so much work. Fellner–Schall
is, historically and mathematically, a **REML variance-component update** (Fellner
1986; Schall 1991), only later recognised (Wood & Fasiolo 2017) as a smoothing-
parameter selector. The quantity it updates is $\sigma_b^2$ when you call the model
a mixed model and $\lambda = \phi/\sigma_b^2$ when you call it a GAM — *the same
number*, by @thm-equivalence and the
[grand equivalence](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence).

This is the punchline for the [GAMM unification](gamm.qmd#sec-unification): a
generalised additive *mixed* model is a GAM with extra penalty blocks (a
random effect is a ridge penalty, $S = I$). Every smooth's $\lambda$ and every
random effect's variance are *the same kind of parameter*, so **one
Fellner–Schall loop selects all of them at once**. There is no separate "variance
estimation" stage bolted onto "smoothing selection"; the
[LME REML loop](lme.qmd#sec-reml) and the GAM smoothing loop are the *same loop*.
The only reason `nitrix`'s dedicated variance-component solver
(`stats/lme/reml.py`) uses Newton–Raphson in $\log\sigma^2$ instead (Lindstrom &
Bates 1990, via the FaST-LMM spectral trick) is that for a *pure* LME with a
shared design the rotated problem becomes a tiny 1-D/2-D smooth optimisation where
Newton's quadratic convergence wins; Fellner–Schall is the tool of choice once a
*penalised basis* is in play and you want positivity and trace-only updates.
Same REML target, two ways up the hill.

::: {.callout-note title="Honest convergence"}
"Increases the marginal likelihood every step" holds under the mild conditions of
Wood & Fasiolo (2017) — chiefly that the Laplace approximation to the marginal
likelihood is accurate, which it is for Gaussian and well-behaved exponential-
family responses. It is *not* a contraction guarantee with a global rate. `nitrix`
takes the engineering stance that matches the GPU: a *fixed* iteration budget
(`n_outer`), clamped $\lambda$, warm-started inner IRLS (`beta0` carried across
outer steps). Empirically this lands on the optimum to plotting accuracy in a
handful of steps (see @fig-fs-convergence), which is all a mass-univariate fit
needs.
:::

## Watching it converge

A self-contained demonstration — pure NumPy, no `nitrix`, no GPU, so it runs at
doc-build time. We fit a single penalised smooth to noisy data and let
@eq-fellner-schall pick $\lambda$ from a deliberately bad start.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fs-convergence
#| fig-cap: "Left: the Fellner–Schall iterates for λ converge in a few steps from a far-off start. Right: the resulting smooth recovers the true function without over- or under-fitting."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 200
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
f_true = np.sin(2 * np.pi * x) + 0.3 * x
y = f_true + rng.normal(0, 0.35, n)

# Gaussian-bump basis + a second-difference roughness penalty S (rank K-2).
K = 30
knots = np.linspace(0, 1, K)
h = 1.0 / (K - 1)
B = np.exp(-0.5 * ((x[:, None] - knots[None, :]) / (1.2 * h)) ** 2)
D = np.diff(np.eye(K), n=2, axis=0)
S = D.T @ D
rankS = np.linalg.matrix_rank(S)
BtB, Bty = B.T @ B, B.T @ y

def fit(lam):
    A = BtB + lam * S
    Ainv = np.linalg.inv(A)
    beta = Ainv @ Bty
    edf = np.trace(Ainv @ BtB)              # effective degrees of freedom
    rss = float(np.sum((y - B @ beta) ** 2))
    phi = rss / max(n - edf, 1.0)           # dispersion
    V = phi * Ainv                          # Bayesian coefficient covariance
    return beta, edf, phi, V

lam, traj = 1.0e4, [1.0e4]                   # start far too smooth
for _ in range(30):
    beta, edf, phi, V = fit(lam)
    num = rankS / lam - np.trace(V @ S)      # tr(S_λ⁻ S) − tr(V S)   [Lemma]
    den = float(beta @ S @ beta) / phi       # γᵀSγ / φ
    lam = float(np.clip(lam * num / den, 1e-6, 1e10))
    traj.append(lam)

beta, edf, phi, V = fit(lam)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.4))
axL.semilogy(traj, marker="o", ms=3)
axL.set(xlabel="Fellner–Schall iteration", ylabel="λ", title="convergence of λ")
axR.scatter(x, y, s=8, alpha=0.35, label="data")
axR.plot(x, f_true, lw=2, label="truth")
axR.plot(x, B @ beta, lw=2, ls="--", label=f"fit (edf≈{edf:.1f})")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8); axR.set_title("selected smooth")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
print(f"converged λ ≈ {lam:.3g}   effective degrees of freedom ≈ {edf:.2f}")
```

From a start four orders of magnitude too stiff, the multiplicative update walks
$\lambda$ down to its marginal-likelihood optimum in a handful of steps and the
fitted smooth tracks the truth — the qualitative behaviour `gam_fit` relies on
when it runs a *fixed* iteration budget over a whole brain.

The convergence picture is clearer still in the coordinate the update is really
trading on: the **effective degrees of freedom**. The
[derivation above](#the-update) built the numerator as "the smoothing the data
used"; EDF is precisely that number. @fig-fs-path starts the iteration from
*both* extremes — far too stiff and far too
wiggly — and plots the $(\lambda,\text{EDF})$ trajectory. Both starts walk to the
*same* fixed-point EDF: the degrees of freedom the data actually support, neither
imposed nor guessed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fs-path
#| fig-cap: "The (λ, EDF) trajectory from two opposite starts. Left: in the (λ, EDF) plane both runs converge to the same fixed-point EDF (dotted). Right: EDF — the 'degrees of freedom the data bought' — stabilises within a few iterations regardless of where you start."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 200
x = np.linspace(0, 1, n)
f_true = np.sin(2 * np.pi * x) + 0.3 * x
y = f_true + rng.normal(0, 0.35, n)

K = 30
knots = np.linspace(0, 1, K)
h = 1.0 / (K - 1)
B = np.exp(-0.5 * ((x[:, None] - knots[None, :]) / (1.2 * h)) ** 2)
D = np.diff(np.eye(K), n=2, axis=0)
S = D.T @ D
rankS = np.linalg.matrix_rank(S)
BtB, Bty = B.T @ B, B.T @ y

def fit(lam):
    Ainv = np.linalg.inv(BtB + lam * S)
    beta = Ainv @ Bty
    edf = np.trace(Ainv @ BtB)
    rss = float(np.sum((y - B @ beta) ** 2))
    phi = rss / max(n - edf, 1.0)
    V = phi * Ainv
    return beta, edf, phi, V

def fs_step(lam):
    beta, edf, phi, V = fit(lam)
    num = rankS / lam - np.trace(V @ S)      # tr(S_λ⁻ S) − tr(V S)
    den = float(beta @ S @ beta) / phi       # γᵀSγ / φ
    return float(np.clip(lam * num / den, 1e-8, 1e10)), edf

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.4))
for lam0, lab in [(1.0e4, "start stiff"), (1.0e-4, "start wiggly")]:
    lam = lam0
    lam_path, edf_path = [], []
    for _ in range(25):
        lam_path.append(lam)
        lam, edf = fs_step(lam)
        edf_path.append(edf)
    axL.semilogx(lam_path, edf_path, marker="o", ms=3, label=lab)
    axR.plot(edf_path, marker="o", ms=3, label=lab)

# Fixed-point EDF (run to convergence) as a reference line.
lam = 1.0
for _ in range(60):
    lam, edf_star = fs_step(lam)
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.axhline(edf_star, color="0.5", ls=":", lw=1)
axL.set(xlabel="λ (log scale)", ylabel="effective d.o.f.",
        title="(λ, EDF) trajectory")
axR.set(xlabel="Fellner–Schall iteration", ylabel="effective d.o.f.",
        title="EDF the data 'bought'")
axL.legend(fontsize=8); axR.legend(fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
print(f"fixed-point EDF ≈ {edf_star:.2f}")
```

This is the right way to *read* a Fellner–Schall fit: not "what is $\lambda$" (a
scale-dependent, hard-to-interpret number) but "how many degrees of freedom did
the smooth spend." The fixed point is where the model's *spent* flexibility equals
the flexibility the data *support*.

## Multiple parameters and disjoint blocks {#multiple-params}

Real fits carry several penalties at once: two or three smooths, plus random-
effect blocks in a GAMM. The thrift of @eq-fellner-schall scales because of
@lem-disjoint: each $\lambda_k$'s update reads *only its own block's* rank and the
$k$-th diagonal block of $V$. The updates therefore **decouple** — one cheap step
per penalty, computed in parallel across penalties (`jax.vmap(fs)` over
`jnp.arange(m)` in `gam.py`), with the only coupling being through the shared
inner solve that produced $V$.

When blocks *do* overlap — a tensor-product smooth's marginal penalties, or a
random-effect covariance with off-diagonal structure — the relevant block of $V$
is no longer a clean per-penalty quantity, and the efficient route is the
[block-Woodbury identity](solvers.qmd#sec-woodbury): update the marginal
covariance $V_y = ZGZ^{\top}+R$ through the low-rank random-effect block without
re-inverting the whole $N\times N$ system. That is what keeps the multi-block
update cheap at scale; `nitrix`'s `_trace_slinv_sk` covers the trace side by
working in block eigenvalues, so even the overlapping case never forms a
pseudo-inverse.

## Where it goes next: Gaussian processes {#gp-extension}

@thm-equivalence keeps paying out. A Gaussian-process smooth with kernel $K$ is
*the same construction* with the penalty $S_k = K^{-1}$ — a GP prior is
another quadratic penalty, and @eq-fellner-schall estimates its variance
unchanged. The amplitude of the GP *is* the inverse smoothing parameter:
$\lambda = \sigma_e^2/\sigma_f^2$, exactly @eq-lambda-ratio with the GP names.

This is no longer hypothetical. In the GP module (`stats/gp.py`, `stats/hgp.py`),
`gp_fit` fits a per-element GP smooth with its **lengthscale estimated**, and the
inner engine is precisely Fellner–Schall. The trick that makes it tractable is the
Hilbert-space approximate GP ([HSGP](hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp); Solin & Särkkä 2020):
expand $f$ in a *fixed* Laplace–Dirichlet eigenbasis $\Phi$ on a bounded domain,
so the kernel enters **only** through diagonal spectral-density weights
$s_j(\rho)$. The penalty becomes
$$
S(\rho) = \operatorname{diag}\!\left(1/s_j(\rho)\right),
$$ {#eq-hsgp-penalty}
a *diagonal* penalty with no eigendecomposition in the loop. So the fit splits
cleanly:

- **Inner** (fixed lengthscale $\rho$): Fellner–Schall selects
  $\lambda = \sigma_e^2/\sigma_f^2$ — the `gam_fit` machinery verbatim, with the
  one diagonal penalty @eq-hsgp-penalty on the smooth block.
- **Outer** (select $\rho$): a 1-D search over $\log\rho$ of the pooled REML
  marginal likelihood, on a fixed log-spaced grid with parabolic refinement.
  $\rho$ is *shared* across elements (one eigenbasis, one smoothness); amplitude
  and noise stay per element — the natural mass-univariate factoring.

So the lengthscale is the one hyperparameter Fellner–Schall does *not* set
directly (it enters the penalty nonlinearly, not as its scale); everything else —
the amplitude, the per-element variances — is the same variance-selection step
this page derived. See the [HSGP chapter](hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp) for the eigenbasis and
the [Bayesian reading](hsgp.qmd#sec-bayes-aside) of the spectral prior.

## Related across the federation

Fitted statistical maps do not live in a vacuum: they are written back through
the on-disk data structure described in the
{{< xref tensorbids:architecture "tensorbids architecture" >}} chapter, which
is how downstream tools rediscover them.

## Further reading

- Fellner, W. H. (1986). *Robust estimation of variance components.*
  **Technometrics** 28, 51–60. — the original multiplicative variance-component
  update REML rests on.
- Schall, R. (1991). *Estimation in generalized linear models with random
  effects.* **Biometrika** 78, 719–727. — extends the update to GLMM variance
  components; the "Schall" of Fellner–Schall.
- Wood, S. N. & Fasiolo, M. (2017). *A generalized Fellner–Schall method for
  smoothing parameter optimization with application to Tweedie location, scale and
  shape models.* **Biometrics** 73, 1071–1081. — the modern form `nitrix`
  implements, with the marginal-likelihood-ascent property.
- Wood, S. N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R*, 2nd
  ed. **Chapman & Hall/CRC.** — the standard reference on penalised GAMs and the
  penalty/variance duality (@thm-equivalence).
- Wood, S. N. (2011). *Fast stable restricted maximum likelihood and marginal
  likelihood estimation of semiparametric generalized linear models.* **J. R.
  Statist. Soc. B** 73, 3–36. — the REML criterion and its stable computation.
- Lindstrom, M. J. & Bates, D. M. (1990). *Newton–Raphson and EM algorithms for
  linear mixed-effects models.* **JASA** 85, 1014–1022. — the Newton route the
  LME solver takes for pure variance components.
- Solin, A. & Särkkä, S. (2020). *Hilbert space methods for reduced-rank Gaussian
  process regression.* **Statistics and Computing** 30, 419–446. — the HSGP
  approximation that turns lengthscale estimation into a diagonal penalty.

## See also

- Code:
  [`nitrix/stats/gam.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gam.py)
  (the Fellner–Schall outer loop, `_gam_fit_one` / `_trace_slinv_sk`),
  [`nitrix/stats/lme/reml.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/reml.py)
  (the Newton REML variant),
  [`nitrix/stats/basis.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/basis.py)
  (penalised bases),
  [`nitrix/linalg/_smalllinalg.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_smalllinalg.py)
  (`small_inv_logdet`, the cuSOLVER-free inner solve).
- Previous rung: [generalised additive models](gam.qmd#sec-penalty) — where the
  penalty and EDF are introduced. Next rung:
  [GAMMs](gamm.qmd#sec-unification) — where one Fellner–Schall loop selects every
  smooth and variance component, and [HSGP](hsgp.qmd#sec-hsgp) — where it sets the
  GP amplitude.
- Shared machinery: the [solvers chapter](solvers.qmd#sec-cholesky) for the
  Cholesky log-determinant/trace identities, and
  [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-equivalence) for the penalty–variance
  equivalence.
